title: Uniforms & Time
description: Static colors are just the beginning. The `u_time` uniform counts seconds since the shader started, and the `sin()` function turns that into a smooth oscillation. Feed time into math and your shader comes alive.

theory.key_concept: `u_time` is a float uniform updated every frame. Wrapping it in `sin()` produces a value that oscillates between -1 and +1. Rescaling with `sin(t) * 0.5 + 0.5` maps that to the [0, 1] range needed for colors.
theory.math_note: Sine oscillates with period 2π ≈ 6.28 seconds at frequency 1. Multiply the argument to speed up: `sin(u_time * 3.0)` completes three full cycles per 2π seconds. Use `abs()` to fold the negative half into positive space.
theory.phase_offsets: To animate RGB channels independently, add a phase offset so each channel is at a different point in the cycle. Offsetting by 2π/3 (≈ 2.094) distributes three channels evenly around the sine wave, producing a smooth colour cycle.

challenges.title: Pulsing red
challenges.description: Make the canvas pulse between black and red over time. The red channel should smoothly oscillate using `sin(u_time)`, remapped to [0, 1].
hints: `sin(u_time)` returns a value in [-1, 1]. To shift it into [0, 1], multiply by 0.5 then add 0.5: `sin(u_time) * 0.5 + 0.5`.
Assign the result to `r` and use it as the red component of `fragColor`.

validation_config.note: Validator checks that the red channel varies sinusoidally with u_time; green and blue must stay 0.

challenges.unlocks_theory: The `* 0.5 + 0.5` rescaling pattern appears everywhere in shader math. It converts a [-1, 1] oscillation into a [0, 1] range safe for colors, blend factors, and lerp weights.

challenges.title: RGB cycle
challenges.description: Cycle through the full colour wheel by driving each RGB channel with a sine wave offset by 2π/3 radians (≈ 2.094). This spreads R, G, and B evenly around the wave so they never peak at the same time.
hints: Start with red: `sin(u_time) * 0.5 + 0.5`. Then add `offset` inside the sine for green and `offset * 2.0` for blue.
The three channels are: `sin(u_time)`, `sin(u_time + offset)`, `sin(u_time + offset * 2.0)` — all rescaled with `* 0.5 + 0.5`.

validation_config.note: Validator samples the canvas at several time values and checks that R, G, B follow phase-offset sine curves.

challenges.unlocks_theory: Phase offsets let you schedule multiple animations on the same wave, producing smooth colour cycling, staggered particle bursts, or interlocked UI pulses without any branching.

challenges.title: Speed control
challenges.description: Create a fast-bouncing pulse that snaps between dim and bright. Use `abs(sin(u_time * 3.0))` — the `abs` folds the negative half up so the pulse hits its peak three times per 2π seconds with no negative dip.
hints: Multiplying `u_time` by a constant scales the frequency: `sin(u_time * 3.0)` completes three cycles in the time `sin(u_time)` completes one.
`abs(x)` returns the absolute value, flipping negative numbers to positive. `abs(sin(...))` never goes below 0, giving a double-humped pulse.

validation_config.note: Validator checks brightness = abs(sin(u_time * 3.0)) and the warm tint ratios (1.0, 0.6, 0.2).

challenges.unlocks_theory: Frequency and speed are controlled by multiplying the time argument. `abs()` is a cheap way to double the pulse frequency and ensure colors stay positive without clamping. These two techniques compose freely.
